sentences.text: I think I was a good kid.
I was listening to my parents.
I never talked back, yeah?
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the youngest one.
And I think my mother spoiled me-- always hugged me, and kissed me, and gave me some extra goodies.
I think I was a good kid.
I didn't make any trouble with my parents.
And it wasn't any problem with me.
In school, I wasn't very good, but I always passed the test.
But I wasn't very good in school.
I  remember that.
Yes, I remember.
I remember when my father was in the service.
My father had a little beard.
And when he came home in the uniform without a beard, I couldn't recognize him.
I was, at that time, about five years old.
I remember that.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remembered when many soldiers went through the city.
And then my mother and my two sisters, they went in the attic.
They hid in the attic because those Romanian soldiers, they was very wild.
And they went to houses.
They grabbed [? women. ?] I didn't understand at that time why they have to hide over there-- just my father and my brother.
And we were in the house.
And father told me not to talk about hiding there.
Later on, I understand why they had to hide.
Were terrible things, what they did, those Romanian soldiers went through the city.
What they did-- it was terrible.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] first, they took away all the food.
They were always hungry.
They took away the food.
And food was very hard to get during the war.
And the terrible thing was they grabbed women, young girls, and took her away.
And so that's how I remember the First War.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember always, always a lot of soldiers, often strange men through the city.
That's what I remember.
And long trains of wagons ran through.
That's what I remember in the First World War.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I remember when my father came home for discharge, because I think after 50 years they discharged that time.
And everybody was so happy.
I remember that.
He came home.
The two wars-- well, the first-- right after the war was inflation.
The money was worth nothing.
I remember as a little boy, five, six-year boy, I went somewhere and on the street corner I found a bunch of money.
And I picked up.
And I brought it home.
And I said, look-- I said to my father, look at this what I found, a bunch of money.
He was laughing.
He said, my child.
That is worth nothing.
And he took it right away.
Yeah, so.
I don't really remember at that time.
I was a child.
I don't remember there was any.
I mean, yeah, many-- from the business, we had many good relationships with Christian people.
So I don't remember there was any anti-Semitism.
Well, we didn't know much.
We just knew so much we knew from the paper.
Or people came back from Budapest or somewhere and heard this.
And so people were wondering what's going to happen.
And in 1940, in April, I was called in to this labor camp.
And from then on till '45, we didn't-- well, I didn't know-- well, this group didn't know anything, because we didn't see a paper.
We never saw a video or something.
We didn't know anything.
Just when we heard something-- I heard this and I  heard-- we didn't know nothing about what was going on.
So we were in-- somewhere in Russia, we were loading-- that was in early '44.
In a railroad station, we were loading railroad cars with long logs.
And a train came in.
Soldiers, Hungarian soldiers, came back from vacation, came back.
And they told us they deported the Jewish people.
We didn't believe them.
We didn't know anything.
We didn't believe them.
Then other people came back from Hungary, other soldiers.
Then this must be true.
We heard the word Auschwitz.
What is Auschwitz?
We didn't know anything about this Auschwitz.
Then we found out it's true.
Going back to before the war, did you or any  of your brothers and sisters marry?
I don't understand.
Was what?
Yeah?
Married?
Oh, married.
Married.
Yeah.
Yes.
My brother and my two sisters were married before the war.
And my brother was married.
Yeah, they had children.
My older sister had three children.
My brother had two.
And my younger sister-- she was married to-- well, six, seven years.
And she had one child-- five years little boy.
Five year old.
Yeah.
Well, we had the business [INAUDIBLE].
We continued just the same.
We were worrying always, worrying.
And we didn't know really anything what was going  on.
Just from the papers, from the papers, that's what we knew.
And we knew not everything was true what the papers said.
And we know there is something else going on from what the papers says.
So we were worrying, always worrying what is going to happen.
But we didn't dream it was going to be a Holocaust, the Jewish people were going to be taken to concentration camps.
We didn't dream about that.
In 1939, did you feel anti-Semitism?
Yes, because in the paper was articles about anti-Semitism.
And we saw in the paper [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pictures and articles about the going on in Austria and Germany.
Don't go.
Don't buy by Jewish people.
And we felt that it was in Hungary, too, in the little town, in our house.
We noticed many people, Christian people, didn't come again.
They went to a Christian store to  buy.
So the business was very much done.
So that's how we felt.
But the people were friendly.
They didn't shout at us.
So that's how we felt this anti-Semitism.
Well, I told you, we were worrying.
We didn't know what is going to happen.
And so really, we didn't know what to do.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So this was a terrible situation-- you know, with those children.
What is going to happen with those children?
Yes?
No.
We didn't thought about it.
We didn't have-- where could we go?
Why should we go?
We didn't think about it was going to be-- they were going to take the Jewish people to a concentration camp.
We didn't think about that.
In 1940, in April, I went to the labor camp.
And since then, I didn't know what was going on.
In the 1940s, I went to the labor camp.
And that was in April.
And in June, I was still in the country in Hungary.
In June, I got a note my father passed away in June.
So I got two days to go  home to the funeral.
And I got to the funeral.
I had to go back.
So we did.
He passed away.
I think at that time, we didn't go with every little thing to the doctor.
If somebody was sick, so he was sick-- tried to take something-- an aspirin or had a stomach trouble-- sodium bicarbonate.
And if somebody had a heart attack, that was their first and their last heart attack.
They couldn't save-- there wasn't a ambulance.
There wasn't a doctor.
They didn't do anything.
Just it happened.
They didn't wake up in the morning.
Go to sleep in the evening and didn't wake up in the morning.
So they were wondering, where is he?
So that's how it happened.
And your mother?
My mother, she passed away in 1936. '36, yeah.
Also, what happened-- that was in February-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] January on Shabbat, [INAUDIBLE] in January .
One Sunday evening, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went next door to my aunt, uncle's house.
And my mother and my father stayed home.
It was in the evening.
And suddenly, the maid-- everybody had a maid-- a young girl came over.
Come home.
Your mother is sick.
So I went home.
My father said, why don't you talk to Dr.
Muskowitz?
Your mother is sick.
So I rang Dr.
Muskowitz.
And he was there in about 15, 20 minutes.
He was maybe two blocks away.
And he came back and said, I am sorry, but your mother is dead.
So that's what happened.
But what happened-- they didn't-- we didn't-- well, she wasn't so healthy.
She was a heavy woman.
She never complained.
But I remember she always had some headache or something always.
But she never complained.
Because these Jewish people, they didn't trust in the army, to the service, to the army, or to the regular [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] service, army service, defense [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] service.
So they had to go to work.
There is nothing we could do.
No, I was not, no.
They didn't accept Jewish people to the military service.
So they had to go-- from 18 to 50.
To the labor camp, everybody.
If you were sick, very sick, weren't able to work, well, go home.
Like my brother-- he was just a few weeks in the labor camp.
And they let him go because he was sick.
He had epilepsy, my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I never knew that over there in the labor camp it happened.
It was-- and they let him go.
How did I leave?
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Well, in the mail came a note.
You have to be there and there in this time and that time.
So I packed a little suitcase and I  had to go over there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was from the military whatever, camp, that note.
So there was a headquarter town, Sarospatak-- however you call, Sarospatak.
And there were several hundred people came together.
And we were there a couple of days.
And we sent groups-- 200 was-- about 200 people were in a group.
They sent to different place to work.
But they needed the defense forces.
Like, they needed wood, or to fix the road, or the railroad to  put down racks, or take off tracks and work in the forest.
So all those people worked for the defense purpose.
Jewish people.
What?
Sarospatak is in Eastern Hungary.
East.
Eastern Hungary.
That was from the military headquarter came.
Hungarian, yeah.
No.
Then when the Germans occupied Hungary, then the Germans took over.
But when the Germans occupied Hungary, they were all the young people in the service.
And they were in the service in the labor camp service.
Then when we went outside the country to Ukraine, then the German took over the command of those people.
We were there about two, three days.
And then we were sent out to this group or to the Carpathian Mountains in a little town, [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't stay in the town.
We were up on the mountains.
And we were working there in the forests and cleaning up big logs and send it down.
There was a railroad.
And we loaded the railroad cars, little cars, and sent that down to the valley over there.
And over there, probably some other group take it to the railroad station.
They loaded the railroad cars over there and wherever they needed it.
It was hard work-- seven days a week.
At that time, we had a commander officer.
He was-- well, he-- I would say he was very nice.
But he expected everybody to do an honest work and not just hang around doing nothing.
He expected everybody to do their work.
And I think everybody did the best that we could.
And we didn't have any trouble.
It was hard work and it was-- we were very tired.
But we had decent food at that time.
We had decent food and enough food.
So it wasn't too bad.
Just we were not free.
I don't understand this question.
To escape?
No.
No.
There wasn't anywhere to go.
No, we didn't try it over there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We just accept that we can do nothing about it.
And we were working.
We were hoping some day it will be over.
Then we went to another part in Hungary.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And over there were a couple of months.
Then we went to--
That was in-- that was a part before the First World War this part belonged to Hungary.
After the war, it became Yugoslavia.
And during the Second War, Hitler [INAUDIBLE] give it back to the Hungarian people.
So it was Hungary again.
They called it Backa.
It was from Yugoslavia.
So we went over there.
For a couple of months we were working over there.
What did you do?
The Yugoslavs, they were building bunkers where they were fighting.
So that's what we took apart.
And we saved the metals.
And they delivered somewhere the metals they used up in the military.
And then in a couple of months, we were over there.
I can't  remember exactly how many months.
But we went to Ukraine.
Don't remember this.
We were always in a move.
We were working some place a couple weeks, a couple months, a couple days and came the order for somewhere we needed people over there.
So we went over there.
And we were working over there again.
So it was always moving.
Where they needed work, we were always behind a German front.
We couldn't.
We couldn't get letters.
We couldn't write letters.
We didn't know anything about our families.
Oh, Backa.
Yeah.
Then we went to Ukraine.
That's in Russian territory behind the German military forces moved.
So we moved after them.
And we did work what needed done.
So mainly on the road.
We fixed the road and the railroad tracks.
I remember one was a different work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A bunch of old military uniform came in-- bloody, torn down.
And we had to sort that.
And all the soldiers that fall down there by death, they took off their clothing And they used over again.
So we had to sort that and give it to another group washed those clothes.
And that was dirty and not very pleasant work.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Mostly we worked.
And then we worked in forests, cutting down big  trees.
Well, the Bryansk Forest.
What condition?
Well, that was very hard.
